description: Mechanic jargon translator — maps common automotive technical terms to plain English explanations. Helps the app turn cryptic mechanic-speak into something any driver can understand.

ABS.plainEnglish: A computer-controlled system that prevents your wheels from locking up during hard braking, letting you steer while stopping. When it activates, you'll feel a rapid pulsing in the brake pedal.
ABS.commonFailures: Wheel speed sensor failure, ABS module failure, reluctor ring damage

AFM.standsFor: Active Fuel Management / Displacement on Demand
AFM.plainEnglish: A system that shuts down half your engine's cylinders when you're cruising to save gas. It's like having a V8 that turns into a 4-cylinder when you don't need the power. Common on Chevy/GMC V8s.
AFM.commonFailures: Lifter failure (collapsing), excessive oil consumption, Check Engine Light

ATF.plainEnglish: The special oil that your automatic transmission uses to shift gears, cool itself, and transfer power. Think of it as the blood of your transmission.
ATF.commonFailures: Dark/burnt fluid = neglected maintenance, slipping transmission

Ball Joint.plainEnglish: A ball-and-socket joint that connects your car's steering knuckle to the control arm. It lets the wheel move up and down AND turn left and right at the same time — like your hip joint.
Ball Joint.commonFailures: Worn = clunking noise, loose steering, uneven tire wear. Failure = wheel falls off.

Blow-Off Valve.plainEnglish: A valve that releases excess boost pressure from the intake when you lift off the throttle. It makes the 'psshh' sound you hear on turbocharged cars. It protects the turbo from pressure surge.
Blow-Off Valve.commonFailures: Vacuum leak, boost leak, compressor surge (if faulty)

Caliper.plainEnglish: The clamp-like component that squeezes your brake pads against the rotor when you press the brake pedal. It's like the clamp on a bicycle's hand brake — but hydraulic instead of cable-operated.
Caliper.commonFailures: Sticking (car pulls to one side), leaking, seized piston

Cam Phaser.plainEnglish: A device on the camshaft that adjusts valve timing on the fly — it helps the engine make more power when you accelerate and run smoother at idle. It's like variable timing on a sprinkler system.
Cam Phaser.commonFailures: Rattling noise (especially on Ford 3.5L EcoBoost), Check Engine Light, reduced power

Catalytic Converter.plainEnglish: A metal box in your exhaust system that uses precious metals (platinum, palladium, rhodium) to convert toxic exhaust gases into less harmful ones. It's the car's air filter for pollution.
Catalytic Converter.commonFailures: Clogging (loss of power, rattling), theft (valuable metals), Check Engine Light

Control Arm.plainEnglish: A suspension component shaped like a wishbone that connects the wheel hub to the car's frame. It allows the wheel to move up and down while keeping it aligned forward and backward.
Control Arm.commonFailures: Worn bushings (clunking, wandering steering), bent arm (from pothole/accident)

CV Axle.plainEnglish: A flexible driveshaft that transfers power from the transmission to the wheels while allowing the suspension to move up and down. The rubber boot protects the grease inside — if it tears, dirt gets in and the joint fails.
CV Axle.commonFailures: Torn boot (clicking when turning), vibration on acceleration, complete axle failure

CVT.plainEnglish: A type of automatic transmission that doesn't have fixed gears. Instead, it uses a belt and two pulleys that can change size infinitely, keeping the engine in its most efficient RPM range. It feels like a rubber band stretching — no shift points.
CVT.commonFailures: Belt slipping, transmission shudder, complete failure (especially Nissan, 2013-2018)

DI.plainEnglish: A fuel injection system that sprays fuel directly into the combustion chamber (not the intake port). This gives better power and fuel economy but causes carbon buildup on intake valves since fuel doesn't wash over them.
DI.commonFailures: Carbon buildup on intake valves (needs walnut blasting to clean), injector failure

DPF.plainEnglish: A filter in the exhaust system of diesel vehicles that traps soot particles. It burns them off periodically in a process called "regeneration" — you will see a warning light if it is clogged.
DPF.commonFailures: Clogging (short trips prevent regeneration), expensive replacement

ECT.plainEnglish: A sensor that measures the temperature of your engine coolant and tells the engine computer. It helps control the fuel mixture, cooling fans, and temperature gauge.
ECT.commonFailures: Faulty reading = poor fuel economy, hard starting, Check Engine Light

EGR.plainEnglish: A valve that recirculates a small amount of exhaust gas back into the intake to lower combustion temperatures and reduce NOx emissions. It's like re-breathing a little of your own air to calm down.
EGR.commonFailures: Carbon buildup = stuck open or closed, rough idle, Check Engine Light

EVAP.plainEnglish: A system that captures fuel vapors from your gas tank and sends them to the engine to be burned instead of releasing them into the atmosphere. It's why your gas cap is so important.
EVAP.commonFailures: Leaks (loose gas cap, cracked hoses), purge valve stuck open, Check Engine Light

Flywheel.plainEnglish: A heavy metal disc attached to the engine's crankshaft that stores rotational energy to smooth out the engine's power pulses. On manual cars, the clutch presses against it. On automatics, the starter motor spins it to start the engine.
Flywheel.commonFailures: Worn teeth (starter grinding), warped surface (clutch chatter)

Head Gasket.plainEnglish: A critical seal between the engine block and cylinder head that keeps oil, coolant, and combustion gases separate. When it fails, these fluids mix — often causing white smoke from the exhaust and milky oil.
Head Gasket.commonFailures: Overheating, white smoke, milky oil, coolant loss, rough running

IAC.plainEnglish: A small valve that controls how much air bypasses the throttle plate when your foot is off the gas. It keeps the engine idling smoothly. When it gets dirty, your idle becomes rough or erratic.
IAC.commonFailures: Carbon buildup = rough idle, stalling, high idle

IWE.plainEnglish: A vacuum-operated system on Ford 4WD trucks that disconnects the front wheels from the drivetrain when 4WD isn't engaged. This improves fuel economy. A vacuum leak causes the front hubs to make grinding noise.
IWE.commonFailures: Vacuum leak (grinding noise from front wheels in 2WD), check valve failure

Knock Sensor.plainEnglish: A sensor that listens for the 'pinging' sound of engine knock (detonation) — when fuel ignites prematurely in the cylinders. It tells the engine computer to retard timing to prevent engine damage.
Knock Sensor.commonFailures: Faulty sensor = reduced power, poor fuel economy, Check Engine Light

MAF Sensor.plainEnglish: A sensor that measures how much air is entering the engine so the computer can add the right amount of fuel. It's a thin wire that gets hot and measures how much air cools it.
MAF Sensor.commonFailures: Dirty sensor = rough running, poor fuel economy. Clean with MAF cleaner spray.

Misfire.plainEnglish: When a cylinder fails to ignite the fuel-air mixture properly. You'll feel it as a hesitation, stumbling, or shaking, especially under acceleration. The Check Engine Light will flash if it's severe.
Misfire.commonFailures: Causes: bad spark plug, faulty ignition coil, vacuum leak, fuel injector problem

O2 Sensor.plainEnglish: A sensor in your exhaust that measures how much oxygen is in the exhaust gases. The engine computer uses this to fine-tune the fuel mixture. There are usually two — one before and one after the catalytic converter.
O2 Sensor.commonFailures: Sluggish response = poor fuel economy, Check Engine Light, failed emissions test

PCV Valve.plainEnglish: A small one-way valve that vents harmful gases from the engine crankcase back into the intake to be burned. It prevents pressure buildup and oil leaks. When clogged, it can cause oil leaks and rough idle.
PCV Valve.commonFailures: Clogged = oil leaks, sludge buildup, rough idle. Cheap and easy to replace.

PTU.plainEnglish: A gearbox that transfers power from the transmission to the rear wheels in AWD vehicles. On Ford Explorers, it's prone to overheating and failure because it shares fluid with the transmission — and the fluid is often neglected.
PTU.commonFailures: Overheating (no dedicated fluid cooler), seal leaks, gear failure ($2k+ replacement)

Rotor.plainEnglish: The metal disc that your brake pads squeeze against to stop the car. It's like a thick metal plate that spins with the wheel. When you brake, the caliper clamps the pads against the rotor.
Rotor.commonFailures: Warped (vibration when braking), worn too thin (needs replacement), rusted

Serpentine Belt.plainEnglish: A single long rubber belt that snakes around several pulleys to drive the alternator, power steering pump, AC compressor, and water pump. It's like a fan belt on steroids.
Serpentine Belt.commonFailures: Cracks, glazing, fraying — if it snaps, you lose power steering, AC, and battery charging

Sway Bar.term: Sway Bar Link / Stabilizer Bar Link
Sway Bar.plainEnglish: A small metal rod with ball joints at each end that connects the suspension to a bar that runs across the car. It reduces body lean when turning. When it wears out, you'll hear clunking over bumps.
Sway Bar.commonFailures: Worn ball joints = clunking noise, excessive body roll in corners

TCM.plainEnglish: The computer that controls your automatic transmission's shifting. It decides when to shift gears based on speed, throttle position, and engine load. Modern cars integrate it with the main engine computer.
TCM.commonFailures: Erratic shifting, limp mode, no reverse, Check Engine Light

Timing Belt.plainEnglish: A toothed rubber belt that synchronizes the rotation of the crankshaft and camshaft, ensuring the valves open and close at the right time. If it breaks on an 'interference engine', the pistons hit the valves and destroy the engine.
Timing Belt.commonFailures: Snapped belt = catastrophic engine damage. Replace per schedule (typically 60k-105k miles)

Timing Chain.plainEnglish: A metal chain (like a bicycle chain but stronger) that does the same job as a timing belt but is designed to last the life of the engine. It's quieter and more durable, but more expensive to replace.
Timing Chain.commonFailures: Stretch (rattling noise), tensioner failure, guide rail breakage

TPS.plainEnglish: A sensor that tells the engine computer exactly how far you've pressed the gas pedal. It's mounted on the throttle body. When it fails, the car may hesitate, surge, or refuse to accelerate.
TPS.commonFailures: Dead spot in sensor = hesitation, surging, Check Engine Light

Turbo Lag.plainEnglish: The slight delay between pressing the gas pedal and the turbocharger building up enough exhaust pressure to deliver boost. It feels like a hesitation before the car 'takes off'.
Turbo Lag.commonFailures: Normal characteristic — excessive lag = boost leak, wastegate issue, or bad turbo

Valve Body.plainEnglish: The hydraulic control center of an automatic transmission — a maze of passages and valves that directs transmission fluid to the right clutches and bands at the right time. It's the 'brain' of the transmission's hydraulics.
Valve Body.commonFailures: Worn passages = harsh shifting, slipping, delayed engagement

VVT.plainEnglish: A system that changes when the intake and exhaust valves open and close depending on engine speed and load. It optimizes power, fuel economy, and emissions across the entire RPM range.
VVT.commonFailures: Oil pressure issues, cam phaser failure, timing chain stretch

Wastegate.plainEnglish: A valve that controls how much exhaust gas flows into the turbocharger. By opening and closing, it regulates the maximum boost pressure the turbo produces. Think of it as a pressure relief valve for the turbo.
Wastegate.commonFailures: Stuck open = no boost, stuck closed = overboost (limp mode), Check Engine Light

Wheel Bearing.plainEnglish: A set of steel balls (ball bearings) inside a metal ring that allow the wheel to spin freely with minimal friction. It's pressed into the wheel hub assembly. When it fails, it makes a droning/humming noise that changes with speed.
Wheel Bearing.commonFailures: Grinding, humming noise, wheel play (dangerous), ABS issues

4WD / AWD.plainEnglish: Systems that send power to all four wheels. 4WD is usually part-time and meant for off-road use (driver selects it). AWD is always on and automatically adjusts power distribution for better traction on any surface.
4WD / AWD.commonFailures: Transfer case issues, differential failure, CV axle wear, PTU overheating (Ford)

EcoBoost.plainEnglish: Ford's name for their turbocharged, direct-injected gasoline engines. They use smaller displacement engines with turbocharging to get V8 power from a V6 (or V6 power from a 4-cylinder).
EcoBoost.commonFailures: Cam phaser rattle (3.5L), carbon buildup, intercooler condensation issues
